text: COUPANG. See COEPANG.
COUPE-LENCH, a township in the p. of Bury, in Lancashire, 4 m. SSE of Haslingden. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. 1,716.
COUPER'S ISLES, a group in the Arctic ocean, at the mouth of the Coppermine river, between the parallels of 67° 50' and 68° 5' N, and to the W of Beren's Isles. They consist of greenstone.
COUPIAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Saint-Sernin, 12 m. W of Saint-Affrique. Pop. 2,763.
COUPLAND, a township in the p. of Kirk-Newton, in Northumberland, 4 m. NW of Wooler. Pop. 109.
COUPTRAIN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, arrond. of Mayenne.-The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1841, 15,267.—The com., 18 m. NE of Mayenne, has a pop. of 538.
COUPVRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lagny. Pop. 616.
COURANTIN. See CORENTYNE.
COURBEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Evron. Pop. 1,105.

COURBEVOIE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine, arrond. of Saint-Denis. —The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1841, 17,241. -The com. and town is 5 m. NW of Paris, on the 1. bank of the Seine, at an alt. of 269 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 6,085.
COURCAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Blévé. Pop. 874. COURCEBŒUFS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Ballon. Pop. 1,133.
COURCELLES, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 4 m. NW of Charleroi. Pop. 3,226. Coal is wrought here.
COURCELLES-CHAUSSY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Pange. Pop. 1,417.
COURCELLES-CAMFLEUR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Bernay. Pop. 191.

COURCELLES-LES-LENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Carvin. Pop. 734. Beet-root sugar is made here. COURCELLES - SUR - AIRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Vaubecourt. Pop. 315.
COURCEMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Ballon, 12 m. NNE of Le Mans. Pop. 1,862.
COURCHATON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Villersexel. Pop. 1,059.
COURCHELETTE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, 2 m. from Douay. Pop. 180.
COUR-CHEVERNY, a commune and town of France, in the arrond. and 7 m. SE of Blois, cant. of Contres. Pop. 1,734.
COUR-SUR-HEURE, a commune and village of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 10 m. SW of Charleroi. Pop. 314.
COURCITE', a commune and town of France, cant. of Villaines-la-Juhel. Pop. 1,809.
COURCO. See KORAKA.
COURÇON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, arrond. of La Rochelle. The cant., comprising 13 com., had a pop. of 13,210 in 1841; the com. of 1,049.
COURÇONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of Villefagnan. Pop. 1,004.
COURCOURY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Saintes. Pop. 1,055.
COURCY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Coutances. Pop. 1,161.
COURDEMANCHE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Grand Luce, 9 m. SW of Saint-Calais, on the r. bank of the Etangsoit. Pop. 1,804. There is a communal college here.
COURGAINS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. of Marolles-les-Braux. Pop. 1,349.
COURGIN, a village of Central Africa, in the Irnanké country, 9 m. E of the frontier of Futa- Diallon. Pop. 150.
COURGIVAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Esternay, 28 m. SSW of Epernay. Pop. 363.

COURLAND, or Kurland [Esthonian, Kurama; SLAVONIAN, Kors, one of the Baltic provinces of Russia, comprising the duchies of C. Proper and Semigallia, and the districts of Pilten and Polangen. It is bounded on the N by the gulf of Livonia; on the E by the provs. of Livonia, Vitebsk, and Minsk; on the S by the prov. of Wilna; and on the W by the Baltic. Its outline is triangular, presenting its base or greatest breadth to the W along the Baltic; and gradually narrowing to its eastern extremity. Its length from WNW to ESE is about 210 geog. m. Its greatest breadth is 150 m. Its superficies is estimated by Hassel at 10,689 sq. m.; in the Weimar Almanach at 23.987 sq. versts or 10,627 sq. m. It is a flat country, interspersed with sand-hills, extensive heaths, and marshes, and patches of cultivated land. It is intersected by two ranges of hills running E and W; but the highest point, the Huningsberg, an alluvial sand-hill, attains an alt. of only 700 ft. The largest lake, the Usmaiten, is about 20 m. in circumf. The principal river is the Düna, with its tributary the Aa. The Windau and the Libau also intersect it. About two-fifths of the surface are covered with noble forests of pine and fir, intermixed occasionally with groves of oak. Agriculture and the rearing of cattle are the leading branches of industry. Corn, flax, hemp, and tobacco are cultivated. The cattle and sheep are not numerous nor of good breeds; but its horses are esteemed throughout Germany and Poland. Game is abundant; and deer, bears, wolves, lynxes, and martins haunt the forests. The only minerals wrought are iron, clay, lime, and gypsum. COU Amber is found on the shores of the Baltic. Manufacturing industry is limited to the fabrication of a few articles of personal and domestic consumption, paper, pottery-ware, and the distillation of spirits.- The pop. in 1826 was 384,790; in 1846, 553,300. The prevailing religion is the Lutheran. The Roman Catholics and Greeks do not possess together above 20 churches.—The land belongs principally to the nobility, who are either of German or of Polish descent. The prov. is divided into 4 over-hauptmannschafft or 'head-captaincies,' viz. Mitau, Selburg, Tuckum, and Hasenpoth; and each of these is subdivided into 2 captaincies. The administrative duties devolve upon a civil governor, who resides at Mitau. In fiscal and military affairs, the prov. is on the same footing as the other provs. of the empire. The ecclesiastical affairs of the Lutherans are managed by a consistory at Mitau. In 1833, the number of public educational establishments was only 62, with 1,636 pupils. There are 11 towns, and 15 market-towns in this prov. The cap. is Mitau. Libau and Windau are the two shipping-ports. History.] The duchy of C. belonged anciently to the Teutonic order, as did also that of Livonia. Gothard Ketler, grand-master of this order, unable to resist the Russians, who attacked and laid waste Livonia, put himself under the protection of Poland, and ceded Livonia to Sigismund Augustus, on condition that he and his successors should retain C. and Semigallia as a hereditary fief to be held of the crown of Poland. At Wilna, accordingly, where this treaty was concluded in 1561, the investiture of the new dukedom was conferred upon Ketler. In 1589, it was enacted by the diet of Poland, that if this fief should be vacated by the extinction of the heirs male of the line of Ketler, the territory held by them should be united to Poland. The republic of Poland was not, however, sufficiently powerful, when that event took place, to enforce its own edict; and C. itself being too small a state to act independently of the great neighbouring kingdoms, the nomination of its dukes, as well as generally the direction of its more important affairs, was regulated by the will of that power which, at each successive period, had most preponderance in the North. So long as Poland was the great ruling power, C. was subservient to that republic. When Sweden, under Gustavus Adolphus and his immediate successors, gained the superiority over Poland, C. was overrun by Swedes, and its sovereign led into captivity The fortune of the house of Vasa having declined, and the ascendancy having passed over to Russia, C. became almost a province of that power; its dukes were elected and deposed, its councils guided by the influence of the court of St. Petersburgh, and its dependence on Poland become no longer anything more than a mere name; but as the influence of Russia in Poland diminished, the duke of C. proceeded to effect the emancipation of himself and his estates from absolute dependence on that court; and the rule of succession was at length established, that the appointment of the dukes should rest with the diet of C., but subject to the approbation of the king and republic of Poland. The constitution of the government of C. was rather of a complicated nature. The crown of Poland, the duke, and the diet, had their respective rights. When Poland was engaged in war, the duke was bound to furnish 200 horse, or 500 infantry; and the nobles 200 horse, or 30,000 dollars, in the first year of the war, provided no Polish or enemy's troops were quartered in the duchy; and in each succeeding year 10,000 dollars. The money of C. was struck of the same standard as the coin of Poland; it also bore on one side the head of the king, or the arms of Poland and Lithuania. All disputes between the duke and his subjects were settled by the king, who received remonstrances from the diet of C. against any infringement of privileges by the duke. Laws tending to alter or new-model the constitution, after having been passed by the duke and diet, required to be finally ratified by the diet of Poland. The Polish diet also confirmed the creation of nobles, and any indigenat recht, or right of naturalization recommended by the duke and diet of C. In all civil causes above the value of 500 Polish florins, and in the specified criminal cases, the final decision belonged to the king and the republic of Poland. On the other hand, the Polish king bound himself to support the constitution of C., to maintain the duke in all his prerogatives, and the nobles and burghers in all their privileges. In any circumstances distinct from those enumerated, and where no contradiction was involved to a distinct feudal dependence on Poland, the supreme authority in the duchy of C. was vested in its own duke and diet. To the duke belonged the executive power, with the general administration of affairs; but though strictly speaking, he had the right to declare war, make peace, and contract alliances, he did not usually proceed on such occasions without consulting the diet. The revenues of the duke were very ample, amounting to not less than £160,000 per annum, derived from the ducal demesnes, in which were comprehended not less than a third of the duchy, and from tolls and customs, manorial and feudal rights, fines, and confiscations. The privileges of the nobles were very great. The highest and most important officers of state were drawn from their body. They were exempted from all taxes and imposts; and any goods or merchandise imported or exported for their use paid no duty. The duke was assisted in the administration of affairs, by what was called the supreme council, consisting of 4 high counsellors, the highsteward, the chancellor, the burgrave, and the marshal. This council advised the duke as to all matters of state. In conjunction also with the duke, it formed the criminal court of judicature for the nobles. After the conquest and final division of Poland, the feudal dependence of C. on that republic was no longer recognised, and the country was annexed to the empire of Russia in 1795.
COURLAND (GULF OF). See KURISCHE-HAFF.
COURLAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sèvres, cant. of Cerisay, 4 m. SW of Bressuire. Pop. 1,688.
COUR-L'EVEQUE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Arcen-Barrois. Pop. 342.
COURLON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Sergines. Pop. 1,166.
COURMANGOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Treffort, 9 m. NE of Bourg. Pop. 1,000.

COURMAYEUR, a village of Piedmont, the highest considerable v. in the valley of Aosta. It is distinguished by the exquisite freshness and purity of its atmosphere, and, as it also possesses mineral springs, it is much frequented by the Piedmontese in summer. It forms an excellent station for a glacierexploring pedestrian. A near cut, across a shoulder of Mont Blanc, may be made from it to Chamouni, distant, by this route, only 13 m.
COURNEUVE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Saint-Denis, 5 m. N of Paris. Pop. 587.
COURMERI, a village of Turkey, in Epirus, on a small stream of the same name which descends from the Chamousi range to the Thyamis.
COURNILLENS, or CURLIN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and bailiwick of Friburg. Pop. 248.
COURNON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. and 5 m. ESE of Clermont-Ferrand, near the l. bank of the Allier. Pop. 2,607.
COURNONTERRAL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Montpellier. Pop. 1,630.

COURONNE (LA), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. and 3 m. SW of Angouleme. Pop. 2,007. Excellent red wines are made here; and there are several paper-mills. The ruins of the celebrated Augustine abbey of La C., founded in 1122, are the finest remains of the class in the Angoumois.

COURONNE (GRAND), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Rouen.—The cant., comprising 12 com., had a pop. of 18,827 in 1841.—The town, 6 m. SW of Rouen, near the l. bank of the Seine, had a pop. of 1,165.
COURONNE (Petit), a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, 5 m. SW of Rouen. Pop. 1,589.

COUROS, a parish and village of Brazil, in the prov. and 140 m. E of Goyaz, district of Santa-Luzia, in the vicinity of Lake Feia.—Also a ramification of the Cordillera-dos-Perinéos, in the same prov., to the E of the districts of Santa-Luzia and São-João-de- Palma, in which the Paranaiva has its source. It forms a table-land sometimes called Chapada-dos- Couros.

COUROUASSA, a village of the territory of Amana, in Central Africa, on the l. bank of the Joliba, 14 m. SE of Baleya. It contains between 400 and 500 in- COU to 12 ft. high. The river here flows from SW 1 S slowly to the ENE. Caillie says that after a heavy rain he found the river flowing here at the rate of about 21 m. an hour, and about 9 ft. deep; and that he was informed it usually begins to overflow in July, when the natives can sail 3 m. over the plain in canoes.

COURPIERES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Thiers.—The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 16,814 in 1841.—The town, 6 m. S of Thiers, on the l. bank of the Dore, is a place of active manufacturing industry. Woollens, pottery and glass ware, and parchment are made here.

COURRENDELIN, or RENNENDORF, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, on the Birs, at the entry of the valley of Moutiers, at an alt. of 1,476 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 991, Catholics.
COURRENSAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. of Eauze. Pop. 1,024.
COURRIE'RES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Carvin, 14 m. ESE of Bethune.
COURROUX, or LUTTELSDORF, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bailiwick of Delemont, on the Birs. Pop. 829, Catholics.
COURS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Thizy, on the Trambouze, 18 m. WNW of Villefranche-sur-Saone. Pop. 4,478. It has cotton and linen weaving establishments.
COURSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Saint-Astier. Pop. 1,213.

COURSAN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, arrond. of Narbonne.— The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. of 9,221 in 1841.—The town is 3 m. NNE of Narbonne, on the r. bank of the Aude. Pop. 2,022.
COURSEGOULES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Grasse.— The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. 3,858.—The com., 9 m. NNE of Grasse, has a pop. of 580.
COURSEL, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, 7 m. NW of Hasselt. Pop. 1,616.
COURSENVILLE, a village of Stillwater township, in Sussex co., in the state of New Jersey, U.S., 83 m. N of Trenton.

COURSEULLES-SUR-MER, a commune and port of France, in the dep. of Caen, cant. of Creully, on the r. bank of the Seule, 1 m. from its embouchure. Pop. 1,445. Lace is manufactured in the town. In 1841, 276 coasting-vessels <math>= 5,952</math> tons, and 6 vessels from foreign parts = 306 tons, entered the port; and 228 coasting-vessels = 4,567 tons, and 12 vessels for foreign ports = 664 tons, left it. The registered tonnage in <math>1842</math> was 50 vessels <math>= 1,187</math> tons.

COURSON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Saint-Sever. Pop. 1,502.—Also a canton and commune in the dep. of Yonne, arrond. of Auxerre. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. 8,123. The town is 11 m. S of Auxerre, and has a pop. of 1,509. Charcoal and hydraulic cement are made here.
COURT, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bailiwick of Moutier, on the river Birs. Pop. 253, Protestants.

COURTABLEAU, a river of Louisiana, U. S., a tributary of the Atchafalaya. It is composed of two head-branches, the Bouf and the Crocodile, which unite in about W long. 91° 58', and flow in a SE course to the r. bank of the Atchafalaya, which it joins on the NW confines of Baton-Rouge. The length of the main stream is about 20 m.

COURTALIN, a commune and village of France, habitants; and is surrounded by a mud wall from 10 | in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Cloyes. Pop. 578.—Also a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Seineet-Marne, com. of Pommeuse. Pop. 180.
COURTEENHALL, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5 m. S of Northampton. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 143.
COURTEILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of Putanges. Pop. 397.

COURTELARI, a parish and town of Switzerland, in the cant. and 24 m. NW of Berne, on the l. bank of the Suse.—The town is the cap. of a bailiwick comprising 7 parishes, and formerly attached to the bishopric of Basle. Pop. 1,224.

COURTENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Morestel. Pop. 1,395. - Also a canton, commune, and town, in the dep. of Loiret .- The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. 8,196. The com. and town, 12 m. ENE of Montargis, on the l. bank of the Bied, has a pop. of 2,567.
COURTHESON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Bedarrides, 18 m. NNE of Avignon. Pop. 3,362.
COURTILS, a commune and port of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Ducey, on the l. bank of the Celune. Pop. 723.
COURTINAY, a large village of Hindostan, in the district and 10 m. NW of Bellary.
COURTINE (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. 7,995. The com., 15 m. S of Aubusson, has a pop. of 842.

COURTISOLS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Marson, on the Vesle, 7 m. NE of Chalons-sur-Marne. Pop. 1,900. The district is distinguished for its agricultural industry; and its pop., descended from a Swiss colony who settled here in the 17th cent., retains a peculiar patois and customs of their own.
COURTIVEON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or, cant. of Is-sur-Tille, 13 m. N of Dijon. Pop. 304.
COURTLAND, a village in Lawrence co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 119 m. W of Tuscaloosa.
COURTMACSHERRY, a fishing village in the p. of Lislee, co. Cork, 21 m. ESE of Timoleague, on the W shore of a bay of the same name. Pop. 740.
COURTOMER, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, arrond. of Alençon. The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 8,177 in 1841. The com. is 14 m. NE of Alençon. Pop. 1,264.
COURTOWN, a small port of co. Wexford, <math>2\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SE of Gorey, at the embouchure of the Owenvarra.

COURTRAY, or COURTRAI, [FLEMISH, Kortryk; DUTCH, Kortrijk, a fortified town of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, the cap. of an arrond. of the same name, 16 m. E of Ypres, and 25 m. S of Bruges, in N lat. 50° 49', E long. 3° 18'. The town occupies both banks of the Lys, by means of which it has communication with the principal towns of Flanders. It is also connected with Bruges by a railway 32 m. in length, executed at a cost of £340,000; and another line from C. to Ypres and Poperinghe has been laid out. Its streets are wide, and the houses are well built, but the general effect is that of "a straggling cheerless town." [Tennent.] It contains several fine edifices; among others the town-hall, a Gothic structure, and the churches of St. Martin and of Notre Dame. in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, in the cant. and 2 m. At either extremity of the bridge which crosses the Lys, in the centre of the town, are two vast circular towers, built in the 15th cent., which serve as the town prisons. It also has an exchange, an academyof-painting, a college, 2 orphan asylums, and a public library of 12,000 vols. A great part of the pop. is employed in spinning flax, and weaving and bleach- Mid-Lothian, 34 m. E of Dalkeith. ing linen cloth and table-linen, which, under the name of Courtray cloth, is sold unbleached in the weekly and parish of Spain, in Galicia, in the prov. of Orense COU market in the town; the pieces being afterwards prepared for sale to the consumers. The number of pieces of linen brought to the town annually amounts to 30,000, about two-thirds of which are bought by the dealers in C.; the remainder is sold to dealers from Bruges, Ypres, Ghent, and Brussels. The cultivation and preparation of flax is pursued with singular skill in the vicinity of C. The flax when cut is carefully sunned and dried, and stored for twelve months before it is submitted to the process of steeping or watering. It is then immersed in the running water of the Lys for 7 or 8 days. Thread-lace, silklace, and flannels, are also among the branches of industry in this place.—The pop. in 1838 was 19,152. C. existed in the time of the Romans under the name of Cortoricum; and as early as the 7th cent. enjoyed the privileges of a municipal city. The fortifications of the town were begun in 1290; but the works were enlarged and perfected, chiefly by the French, who built a citadel in 1647. The Flemings, in 1302, commanded by John, count of Namur, encountered a French army near to C., and found on the field, after the battle, about 4,000 gilt spurs, whence the engagement is known in history as 'the battle of the spurs.' A little chapel still marks the spot which was the centre of the action. The town was taken by the French successively in 1643, 1646, 1667, and 1683, and restored to Spain by the peace of Ryswick. The French destroyed the fortifications in 1744; in 1793 they obtained a victory over the English near the town, of which they took possession, and constituted it the cap. of the dep. of the Lys.
COURTS ISLES, a cluster of small islets off the SW point of Bruné isle, to the S of Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 43° 30′, E long. 147° 18′.
COURTWRIGHT, a village in Fairfield co., in Ohio, U. S., 20 m. SE of Columbus.

COURVILLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Chartres.—The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. 9,889.— The town, on the I. bank of the Eure, 9 m. W of Chartres, has a pop. of 1,445.
COURZIEU, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Vaugneray, 15 m. W of Lyon. Pop. 1,624.
COUSANCE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Beaufort, 16 m. SSW of Lonsle-Saunier. Pop. 1,381. It has a considerable trade in fowls, and contains some quarries of marble.

COUSANCES-LES-COUSANCELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Meuse, cant. of Ancerville. Pop. 1,123. It has some iron-works. COUSCEA, Couseca, or Koseea, a town of Upper Guinea, cap. of the kingdom of Kotchi, on the l. bank of the Gallinas, 70 m. NNE of the entrance of that river into the Atlantic. It is surrounded by walls; and contains a pop. variously estimated at from 15,000 to 20,000. Gold is found in the environs.
COUSE, the name of several small rivers of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or.
COUSERANS, or Conserans, formerly a district and bishopric of France, in the prov. of Upper Gascogny, now comprised in the dep. of Ariège, and of which the chief town was Saint-Lizier.

COUSIN, a river of France, which takes its rise W of Sanlieu; runs NW under the names of Saint- Didier and La Roche; passing Bouvray, it enters the dep. of Yonne, flows past Avallon, and unites with the Cure on the r. side, at the village of Blaunay after a total course of 36 m.
COUSLAND, a village in the p. of Cranston,
COUSO-DE-ABION (SANTA-MARIA-DE), a town and partido of Rivadabia, in a mountainous district, and on the confines of a valley of the same name. Pop. 1,200. It has a church and a custom-house.
COUSOLRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Solre-le-Château, 15 m. NE of Avesnes. Pop. 1,220. It has extensive marble and iron-works, and several potteries and tile-works.
COUSPAU, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Drôme, which runs N from the Montagne-d'Angèle, of which it is a ramification.

COUSSAC-BONNEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. and 6 m. E of Saint-Yrieix. Pop. in 1846, 2,803. It has a manufactory of porcelain, and several wire-mills, and contains beds of kaolin, pitunse, and antimony.
COUSSAN, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. and com. of Marmande. Pop. 1,200.
COUSSAY-LES-BOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Pleumartin, 12 m. E of Châtellerault. Pop. 1,310.
COUSSERGUES, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Laissac and com. of Crúejouls. Pop. 1,500.

COUSSEY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, arrond. of Neufchâteau. The cant. comprises 26 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,915; in 1841, 9,278.—The town is 4 m. N of Neufchâteau, on the r. bank of the Meuse. Pop. 831.
COUTAIS. See LUMINE-DE-COUTAIS.

COUTANCES, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche. The arrond., comprising an area of 182,380 hectares, contains 10 cant., viz.: Bréhal, Cérisy-la-Salle, Coutances, Gavray, La-Haye-du-Puits, Lessay, Saint- Malo-de-la-Lande, Montmartin-sur-Mer, Periers, Saint-Sauveur-Landelin. Pop. in 1831, 136,847; in 1836, 135,980; in 1846, 132,857.—The town is situated on a hill, at the confluence of the Soulle and Bulsard, about 9 m. from the sea, 19 m. WSW of St. Lo, and 47 m. S of Cherbourg, in N lat. 49° 2′ 54", W long. 1° 26′ 50″. Pop. in 1789, 5,824; in 1821, 8,790; in 1831, 8,957; and in 1841, 7,920. It has a cathedral—a fine Gothic structure—a college, a public library, a theatre, a chamber of commerce, baths, a printing establishment, &c. Its manufactures, consisting in varieties of woollen and cotton fabrics, tape, lace, parchment, and earthenware, are considerable; and it has an active trade in horses, cattle, fowls, grain, lint, wax, madder, eggs, butter, and articles of local manufacture.
COUTARNOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of L'Isle-sur-le-Serein. Pop. in 1846, 421. It has extensive quarries of a hard species of stone used in the construction of vases.
COU-TCHIN-KEOU, an island of the Yellow sea, near the E coast of China, prov. of Shan-tung, 25 m. SE of Kiao, in N lat. 35° 55'.

COUTERNE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of La-Ferté-Macé, 14 m. ESE of Domfront, in a fine plain, on the l. bank of the Vee, near its confluence with the Mayenne. Pop. in 1846, 1,701. It has manufactories of linen, sacking, and tape. Lint is its chief article of trade. Granite of fine quality abounds in the locality.
COUTERNON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, cant. and 6 m. E of Dijon, on the Norges. Pop. 397. It has an extensive chemical work.
COUTHUIN-RIPPE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 5 m. W of Huy. Pop. 2,114.
COUTICHES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Orchies, 10 m. NE of Douai. Pop. 2,102. It has a manufactory of sugar from beet-root. COV
COUTOUVRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Perreux, 8 m. NE of Roanne. Pop. 1,702.

COUTRAS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, arrond. of Libourne. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,361; in 1841, 11,308. — The town, the ancient Cotracum, is situated on the l. bank of the Dronne, at the confluence of the Ille, 11 m. NNE of Libourne, and on the railroad from Paris to Bourdeaux. Pop. in 1841, 3,302. It has several mills; possesses an active commerce in flour, brandy, and timber. A horse and cattle fair is held monthly.—A victory was gained here by Henry IV. over the Leaguers in 1587.

COUTURE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Saint-André, 16 m. SE of Evreux. Pop. 396. It is noted for its wind-instruments, in the manufacture of which 150 workmen are employed in this com.—Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, cant. and 5 m. NE of Bethune. Pop. 2,356.
COUTURE - D'ARGENSON (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. of Chef-Boutonne, 17 m. S of Melle. Pop. 525.
COUTURES-SUR-GARONNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Meilhan. Pop. 1,289.

COUVES, two islands of Brazil, about 8 m. E of the bay of Ubatuba, in the prov. of São-Paulo. The larger, which lies in S lat. 23° 25′ 54", is woody, affords good water, and on the N side has a commodious bay.
COUVET, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 15 m. WSW of Neufchâtel, in a fine situation on the l. bank of the Reuse, in the Val-de- Travers. It is noted for its lace manufactures.

COUVIN, a commune and town of Belgium, cap. of a canton, in the prov. and 35 m. SSW of Namur, and 15 m. S of Philippeville, on the Eau Noire. It has manufactories of iron and cloth, and a tannery; and in the environs are extensive mines of iron and iron-works.
COUVORDEN (CAPE), a promontory of Russian America, at the S extremity of the peninsula which forms the W side of Lynn canal, and opposite Chatham strait, in N lat. 58° 12'.
COUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Privas. Pop. 1,265.—Also a commune in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Dordogne, cant. of Saint-Cyprien, 15 m. WSW of Sarlat. Pop. 1,583.
COUZE-SAINT-FRONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Lalinde. Pop. 845. It has numerous paper-mills.
COUZEIX-LE-PETIT-LIMOGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. and 4 m. N of Limoges. Pop. 1,355.

COUZON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhône, cant. of Neuville-sur-Saône, 6 m. N of Lyon, on the r. bank of the Saone. Pop. 1,089. It has extensive quarries of freestone, and affords fine specimens of rock crystal.
COVA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 18 m. NNE of Viseu.

COVARRUBIAS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 25 m. SSE of Burgos, partido and 10 m. E of Lerma, on the r. bank of the Arlanza, which is here crossed by a stone-bridge. Pop. 894. It has a church, college, and custom-house, and considerable manufactures.

COVE, or COVE-OF-CORK, a charmingly-situated town and sea-port on the S side of Great island, partly in the p. of Clonmel, but chiefly in that of Templerobin, co. Cork. It overlooks the spacious basin of Inner Cork harbour, 3 m. SE of Passage, and 8 m. ESE of Cork. But its distance from Cork, both by steam-conveyance down the Lee and by railway, is 11 m. The coast of the island, both at the site of the town and in its environs. rises steeply from the water in a picturesquely-featured ascent of a hill. The town-which has recently, in commemoration of the Queen's visit, assumed to itself the appellation of Queenstown-occupies the steep S face of a hill, and rises, terrace above terrace, from near the edge of the water to a considerable height in the acclivity. The quay, besides serving the purposes of commerce, forms a charming promenade. The yacht club of Cork, though taking designation from the city, flings most of its interest, its scenes, and its advantages, around Cove, and has long been a main cause of very intimately and highly associating the name of that town with whatever is attractive in yachting. The weekly exhibitions of the yacht club, the annual regatta, the occasional arrival of the fleet, the magnificence of the circumjacent scenery, and the picturesqueness of the town itself, attract great crowds of gay visitors during summer; and the salubrity of the climate, the comparative equality of the temp., the dryness of the air, and even the power of fame and fashion, bring hither not a small number of consumptive patients, and other classes of invalids. During 20 days of August, 1836, no fewer than 20,479 persons passed over to C. by the ferry at Passage; while possibly a proportionably large number visited it through the other approaches, particularly down the river and by way of Foaty. Mr. Windele, in order to show the comparatively slight variation of temperature which is experienced at C., and consequently how preferable a retreat for invalids this place is to some of the boasted localities on the continent of Europe, states, that, in 1833-4, the mean highest and the mean lowest range of the therm. was in April 53° and 47°, in May 63° and 50°, in June 63° and 50°, in July 70° and 56°, in Nov. 57° and 47°, in Dec. 52° and 46°, in Jan. 49° and 43°, and in Feb. 50° and 43°.—Previous to the North American war of independence, Cove consisted of little more than the mud cabins of a few fishermen. In 1752, John Wesley recorded that "there was nothing to be bought there, -neither flesh, nor fish, nor butter, nor cheese;" and adds, that he was obliged to be well-contented with some eggs and bread. Even so late as 1797, it was summarily characterized as "a wretched dirty place, inhabited chiefly by sailors and fishermen." C. is indebted for its prosperity to its being made the naval station of an Admiral's flag.-Cove gives name to a parish in the Roman Catholic parochial arrangement. Pop. in 1831, 6,966; in 1841, 5,142.—Also a village in the p. of Ringcurran, co. Cork, on the E shore of Kinsale harbour, 14 m. E of Kinsale. Pop. 352.— Also a tything in the p. of Yately, in the co. of Southampton, 9 m. ENE of Odiham. Pop. 433.—Also a fishing-village in the co. of Kincardine, 4 m. SSW of Aberdeen. Pop. 421.
COVE (East), a harbour on Deal island, in Kent group, in Bass' strait.
COVE (NORTH), a parish in Suffolk, 21 m. SSE of Beccles. Area 1,910 acres. Pop. 219.
COVE (South), a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. N of Southwold. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 194.
COVE-CREEK, a village in Benton co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 131 m. ESE of Tuscaloosa.
COVEDO, a town of Austria, in Illyria, 15 m. SE of Trieste.
COVE-HYTHE, or NORTH HALES, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. N of Southwold. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 186.
COVEJA, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. NE of Toledo.

COVELONG, a town of Hindostan, in the Carna- and ribbons,—a trade introduced here, as well as at COV tic, 24 m. S of Madras. It was formerly fortified, but its works were destroyed by the English after the fall of Chingleput.
COVENEY, a parish in Cambridge, 4 m. WNW of Ely. Area 8,420 acres. Pop. 1,523.
COVENHAM-ST.-BARTHOLOMEW, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. NNE of Lincoln. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 277.
COVENHAM-ST.-MARY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 7 m. W of Saltfleet. Area 950 acres. Pop. 169.

COVENTRY, a city and county of itself, locally situated in the co. of Warwick; 9 m. NNE of Warwick; 17 m. E by S of Birmingham, and 91 m. NNW of London; nearly in the centre of England, and equidistant from its four principal ports, London, Liverpool, Hull, and Bristol. The London and Birmingham railway has a station within a few hundred yards of the city. Its local advantages are also multiplied by its almost universal provincial communication through a most extensive system of inland navigation by canals, beginning with the Coventry canal, on the one hand, and its junction with the Birmingham and Grand Trunk canals; and, on the other, with the Oxford canal, and its connection with the Wilks and Berks canal. One of the principal coach-roads between London and Birmingham passes through this city. The town is pleasantly situated on a gentle declivity, bounded for a short distance on the NE by the Sherbourne river, and the Radford brook, which, running from N to S, here unites its waters with the Sherbourne. The most striking feature in the approach to C., is the fine effect of its beautiful tapering spires. The streets are generally narrow, and are darkened and almost closed over by high projecting richly ornamented gable ends and upper stories. Many of them, however, have been replaced by modern ones. Pop. in 1801, 16,034; in 1831, 27,070, of whom 4,913 families were employed in manufactures, trade, &c., and 204 in agriculture; in 1841, 30,743, of whom 2,669 were weavers, 1,855 silk manufacturers, 1,811 ribbon-makers, 831 clock and watch-makers, and 122 silk-dyers.—The county of the city includes an area of about 7 m. in length, and 20 m. in circumf., containing 15,070 acres, and 8 parishes, or parts of parishes—exclusive of those within the limits of the city—namely, Austey, Exhall, Foleshill, Stivichall, Stoke, Wyken, part of St Michael's, including the hamlets of Keresley and Radford, and part of Sow. It is co-extensive with the parliamentary borough. Pop. in 1841, 41,387. C. early acquired affluence and reputation as a place of manufacture and trade. Cloth, caps, and bonnets had become articles of important traffic about the commencement of the 15th cent. For the security of its mercantile consequence, walls were built, from the proceeds of 27 years' accumulation of tolls made for the purpose; after which, the merchants became enriched, the town flourished, and the costly steeple of St. Michael's was built. In the early part of the 16th cent. C. became famous for the manufacture of a blue thread; but the art was lost before 1581, and it was stated by John Hales, to the Protector Somerset, that "trade grew so low, and there was such a dispersion of people from the city, that there were not above 3,000 inhabitants, whereas there had formerly been 15,000." But at least one staple branch of commerce—the woollen and broad cloth manufacture—was now advantageously cultivated, and continued to thrive here till the destruction of the Turkey trade in 1694. The manufacture of striped and mixed tammies, camblets, callimancoes, and shalloons, flourished during the greater part of the 18th cent.; but it is now almost dwindled away, and has been succeeded for about a cent. by the throwing of silk, and the weaving of silk gauze, broad silks. Spitalfields, by French refugees. The manufacture of watches, which was not carried on to any great extent till within the last 40 or 50 years, has also greatly increased. The ribbon manufacture was at first confined to but a few hands, but it has spread to so great an extent, that, in 1838, there were in the C. trade, 3,504 plain engine-looms, only 50 of which were in the rural weaving parishes, including Nuneaton, Bulkington, Bedworth, Chilverscoton, and Hartshill, as well as the outlying parishes of the city and county; and 2,228 jacquard engine looms, of which 950 were in the rural parishes, and 7,480 single-hand looms, all of which were in the rural parishes, except 129 in the city; making a total of 13,229 looms worked by hand, and nearly all employed. Between 17,000 and 18,000 persons, of whom between 6,000 and 7,000 were in the city and suburbs, and the remainder in the rural parishes, were engaged in the trade. In the city and suburban villages the numbers employed were 3,222 plain engine; 1,182 jacquard; 123 single hand; in all, 4,527 looms; besides 53 power and 4 alabar looms. The total number of looms, in 1831, was 14,602; but only 5,062 were employed; of which 2,464 were in the city and suburbs. The total number in the city, in 1818, was 3,268, employing 4,973 hands. The consequences flowing from the admission of French and other foreign goods, have led the trade here, as elsewhere, to desire a renewal or re-establishment of some prohibitory system; but the commissioners, in their interesting report on hand-loom weaving, observe, that "though great distress was felt for a brief period on the introduction of foreign silks, and the highest wear of ribbons is still supplied by France, yet the great body of the trade is now confessedly exempt from the immediate pressure of foreign competition;" that the trade, which has not been so much "depressed as outstripped," has now met with "a rivalry much nearer home than St. Etienne," in the home-competition, and in the factory and steam power manufacture at Congleton, Leek, Derby, &c., and C. itself, where there are several steam-loom factories; and that, in fact, "since the full encounter with French competition, in 1829, the trade, always subject to depressions, has gradually revived and extended, in channels collateral with those in which French goods keep possession of the market." What is called the Great show-fair of C. continues for eight days, commencing on the Friday after Corpus Christi day, Trinity week. Races are held in March. The municipal commissioners who reported on C. in 1835, observe, that if the great antiquity of this city, and its long established importance as a manufacturing town, its central position, &c., &c., be taken into consideration, it seems clear, that its prosperity has not progressed in proportion to its numerous advantages.—C. was till recently comprehended, as an archdeaconry, in the dio. of Lichfield and Coventry, and it even, at various times, took precedence of the bishopric of Lichfield; though, latterly, the titular dignity of the see was Lichfield and Coventry; but, by order of council, 22d Dec., 1836, it was transferred to the dio. of Worcester. The church of St. Michael, not unaptly called the boast of C., is an ancient and splendid Gothic edifice, with a fine tower about 136 ft. high, richly ornamented, and surrounded by a beautiful octagonal tapering spire, 130 ft. high. This church was styled by Sir Christopher Wren a masterpiece of architecture. The interior contains nothing of interest. It was erected in 1373-95, at the charge of two brothers, both mayors of C. The church of the Holy Trinity was a venerable and magnificent pile of building, with a beautiful turret spire; but it was taken down and rebuilt, and the spire repaired, in 1832. The county-hall is a modern structure, erected, in COV 1784, upon the site of the ancient guild hall of the city. St. Mary's hall is a structure of great antiquity, and presents a relict of that munificence which this borough has displayed in by-gone days. It was originally erected in the time of Henry VI. as a place of meeting for the Trinity guild; but it is now used for the festivities of the corporation, and occasionally as a theatre.—The charities of this city are very ancient and numerous as well as largely endowed. The greater part of them have been founded by members of the corporation, who, till 23d February, 1837, when trustees were appointed, had the administration of charity funds to the amount of about £7,200 per annum. The total annual income of charities connected with C., in 1836, was £10,329 2s. 1d.—Under the municipal act, the city and co. of C. is divided into 6 wards; and is governed by 12 aldermen and 36 councillors. The city returns 2 members to parliament. The number of electors registered for 1836 was 3,662, of whom only 567 were newly privileged electors. The number in 1847 was 4,056. The income of the borough, in 1846, was £17,073. History.] The early accounts of this interesting city are involved in obscurity, until the time of Edward the Confessor, in the early part of whose reign, Leofric and his Countess Godiva, re-founded the famous convent of nuns, whence C. is supposed to have derived its name. Remarkable for its antiquity, charters, rights and privileges, and for the favours bestowed upon it by different monarchs, C. is also remarkable for having had two parliaments held in it:-one by Henry IV., from which all lawyers were excluded, hence called 'the lack learning parliament,' or parliamentum indoctorum; the other by Henry VI., in which Richard, duke of York, and the friends of the house of York were attainted. In 1564, the plague committed dreadful ravages in this city. Mary, Queen of Scots, was twice imprisoned here. Charles I. attempted to possess himself of the city, but it was secured by Lord Brooke for the parliament, by whom it was garrisoned through the war. The women of C. organized themselves on this occasion, and were headed by an amazon, who led them daily forth to useful service. After the restoration, Charles IL sent the Earl of Northampton, with a body of troops, to break down the city walls, as a punishment for the citizens having shut their gates upon his father, which orders they so effectually executed, that out of walls 3 m. in circuit, and flanked with 32 embattled towers, they left nothing but the dismantled gates and a few fragments of wall.

COVENTRY, a township in Orleans co., in the state of Vermont, U.S., 54 m. N by E of Montpelier, bounded on the N by Lake Memphremagog. Pop. 786.—Also a township in Grafton co., in New Hampshire, 12 m. ESE of Haverhill, intersected by the Wild Amonoosuck river. Pop. 413. Moosehillock mountain, in the SE part of this t., attains an alt. of 4,636 ft. above tide-water.—Also a township in Tolland co., in Connecticut, 17 m. E of Hartford. Pop. 2,018.—Also a township in Chester co., in Pennsylvania, on Tioga river. Pop. 2,620.—Also a township in Kent co, in the state of Rhode Island, 13 m. SW of Providence. Pop. 3,433.—Also a township in Chenango co., in the state of New York, 114 m. WSW of Albany. Pop. 1,681.—Also a township in Summit co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,308.
COVERHAM, a parish in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SW of Middleham. Area 22,120 acres. Pop. 1,254.
COVERIPAUK. See CAVERYPAUK.
COVERT, a township in Seneca co., in the state of New York, 176 m. W of Albany, intersected by several streams which flow into Cayuga lake. Pop. 1,563.
COVES (ISLE OF), an island in Lake Huron, at the entrance to the Georgian bay, 6 m. from Cape Hurd.
COVE-SPRING, a village in Ralls co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 91 m. NNE of Jefferson.
COVILHA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 14 m. SW of Guarda, at the foot of Mount Estrelha. Pop. 6,350. Woollens are manufactured here. In the environs are thermal springs.
COVINGTON, a parish in Huntingdonshire, 31 m. NNW of Kimbolton. Area 1,290 acres. Pop. 142.

COVINGTON, a parish in the upper ward of Lanarkshire, stretching along the Clyde, which bounds it on the E and separates it from Libberton. Pop. in 1801, 456; in 1841, 520. It comprises the two v. of Thankerton and Covington.

COVINGTON, a county in the S part of the state of Alabama, U. S., intersected by the Conecuh river and its tributaries. Area 1,664 sq. m. Pop. 2,435. The cap. is Montezuma.—Also a county in the S part of Missouri, intersected by Leaf river, a tributary of the Pascagoula. Area 960 sq. m. Pop. 2,717. The cap. is Williamsburg.—Also a township in Wyoming co., in the state of New York, 241 m. W by N of Albany. Pop. 2,438.—Also a village in Covington township, Pennsylvania, on a branch of Tioga creek. Pop. 940.—Also a township in Luzerne co., in Pennsylvania, intersected by the Lehigh river. Pop. 596.—Also the cap. of Alleghany co., in Virginia, on Jackson's river, 196 m. W of Richmond. Pop. 300.—Also the cap. of Newton co., in Georgia, 61 m. NW of Milledgeville. In the neighbourhood is Emory college, founded by the Wesleyan Methodists in 1837. It had 4 professors and 55 students in 1849.—Also the cap. of St. Tammany p., in Louisiana, 45 m. N of New Orleans.—Also a v. in Tipton co., in Tennessee, 185 m. WSW of Nashville.—Also a town in Kenton co., in Kentucky, 85 m. SE of Frankfort, on the Ohio river, directly opposite Cincinnati. Pop. 2,026.—Also a v. in Newbury township, in Miami co., in Ohio, 77 m. W of Columbus, on the Saltwater river. Pop. 331.—Also a town in Fountain co., in Iowa, 74 m. WNW of Indianapolis.

COVOLO, or Kofel, a defile between the frontier of the Lombardo-Venetian prov. of Belluno and that of Vicenza, 12 m. WSW of Feltre, in N lat. 45° 56' 37". It is traversed by the Brenta, and by one of the great routes from Tyrol to Italy. In 1796 the Austrians were defeated here by the French.
COW, an island of co. Cork, 1 m. NW of Dorsey island.—Also an islet on the W side of Lough Derg, midway between Portumna and Killaloe.
COW-BAY, an anchorage on the coast of East Falkland island, 2 m. S of Cape Carysfort. It affords good clean anchorage in 7 to 8 fath., but open to the E.
COW-BAY POINT, a headland on the S coast of Jamaica, in N lat. 17° 52′ 35", W long. 76° 39′ 42".
COW AND BULL, two very conspicuous rocks off the NW side of the island of Eleuthera, in the Bahama group, in N lat. 25° 26' 15".
COW-CREEK, a village in Saline co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 72 m. NW of Jefferson.
COW-PASTURE, a river of Virginia, U. S., flowing into James' river from the N.
